steps.prompt: Which words are the finite verbs?

hints.ascende only: 'Erit' is also a finite verb — future of 'esse'
hints.erit only: 'Ascende' is also a finite verb — an imperative
hints.Amice and gloria: 'Amice' is a vocative noun and 'gloria' is a nominative noun — neither is a verb

steps.reveal: ascende = go up! / move up higher! (2nd sg. imperative)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 1st sg. would be 'ascendo' — 'ascende' is a command
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'ascendam/ascendas' — 'ascende' is imperative
hints.infinitive: Infinitive would be 'ascendere' — 'ascende' is the imperative

hints.accendo, accendere, accendi, accensum: These are the parts of 'accendere' (to kindle)
hints.scando, scandere, scandi, scansum: These are the parts of 'scandere' (to climb) — the simple verb
hints.descendo, descendere, descendi, descensu: These are the parts of 'descendere' (to go down) — the opposite

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'erit' is future indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'amicus' — 'Amice' with -e is the vocative of 2nd declension
hints.adverb: 'Amice' could be an adverb meaning 'in a friendly way,' but here it addresses a person

hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'te' — 'tibi' is distinctly dative
Ablative would also be 'gloria' (1st decl.), but here it is the subject — nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'gloria' but one does not address 'glory' here

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gloriae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'gloriae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'gloriae' ends in -ae

hints.positive degree adverb: Positive degree would be 'supra' or 'super' — 'superius' is comparative
hints.noun meaning 'upper room': 'Superius' is an adverb here, not a noun

translation: Friend, go up higher; then there will be glory for you
